segments.text: Good morning-
Good morning
... everybody. It's very good to have you here.
Karen Osborne,
Hayden Waino and Dr. Barbara Disley.
I'll let you introduce yourselves as well, but I understand we have you here for
two hours today.
I'll let you open up with some introductory remarks after introduction, and then
I'll have the two members to ask questions. Over to you.
Thank you for making the time.
Thank you. Tēnā koe, e te tiamana, koutou nga mangai o
te komiti nei. Me ana ra i roto nga tini
āhuatanga o te wā. Thank you again for the opportunity to
allow us to present our last year's work and
perhaps a look
into what we're looking into in the future.
As many of you will know that we were set up
following the inquiry into mental health and addiction back in
2018, and we came into form in February
So,
six years
of existence.
At the time, the commission was set up to be a force for change, helping to lead a
shift
to a mental health wellbeing system that is accountable
and is experienced as joined up, respectful, responsive by tāngata
whai ora,
their families, and whānau.
The system is serving a population now that is poorer in mental
health and wellbeing, an increasing need for mental health support
compared to six years ago.
There have been improvements. More staff in place due to filling vacancies
has been a key driver to improve access rates and reduce wait times.
Over 236,000 people have seen the
Access and Choice program in 2024, but less than the
expected target of 325,000 per
annum. However, for young people,
access to specialist mental health services reduced.
They faced longer wait times, had more referrals declined.
This is particularly concerning given the rate of psychological distress
that is increasing for young people.
In 2024 and '25
was the coming of age for Te Hiranga Mahara.
Our system performance monitor showed a system view linking outcomes
for people with contributions
from the government.
Our first system performance monitoring report outlined what a good mental health
and addiction system looks like.
The report shows that while there are pockets of positive change,
there is a significant way to go to achieve the outcomes people expect.
Our annual stakeholder engagement survey told us there is an overall
positive perception of Te Hiranga Mahara.
Stakeholders rated us highly on our engagement,
reliability of advice, reports, and information that we
publish, and speaking up on important mental health, addiction, and
wellbeing issues.
Māori,
iwi, and lived experience stakeholders report positive engagement with
us.
We can see the impact of our work in three key areas:
the Access and Choice program,
the work to repeal and replace the Mental Health Act,
and the new Mental Health and Wellbeing Strategy.
The launch of the Access and Choice program represented a major
investment in mental health and addiction.
Our monitoring has provided transparency and accountability.
In response to our findings, we saw greater investment in targeted
action and improvement in data quality and
visibility.
Since our establishment, we've advocated for a mental health and addiction
system that places people and whānau at the center.
Central to this has been our support for the work to repeal and replace the
Mental Health Act.
We support legislative and practice changes that uphold
people's rights to make informed decisions about their care and
treatment.
We've advocated for stronger recognition of mental health and wellbeing
within legislation.
Our evidence-based advice made the case for a dedicated mental health
and wellbeing strategy anchored in research and shaped by the voices of
people with lived experience.
Our engagement has been acknowledged and actively sought with the
Minister for Mental Health supporting our ongoing involvement in the
development of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Strategy.
In the coming year ahead,
as our 25/26 is our first year under our
new strategy
and statement of intent.
We have new strategic priorities focused on mental health and addiction services
for tāngata whai ora
and outcomes for people with lived experience,
and on improving the performance of the mental health and addiction system.
We're focusing our efforts over the next three years through making
three strategic shifts on how we work,
placing people with lived experience at the center of our work,
being future-oriented, promoting alignment and leveraging
knowledge to influence positive change,
and being a bold advocate.
The first Hauora Heringa Ro Te Ara Tūroa forum was
a success with over 240 attendees.
Feedback was positive, particularly about the timely announcement
regarding crisis response funding.
Most delegates surveyed found the program relevant, topical and
useful.
The publication of Urupare Morerere Crisis
Response Monitoring
Report was a significant milestoneAnalysis
of media cover showed 23 articles across major outlets in an
estimated reach of 2.8 million impressions.
We published the assessment of progress implementation of Ko Tima Te
Haringa recommendations.
This is the first time that we have assessed progress with recommendations
that we've made.
Of the five recommendations made in early 2024, three have
been completed by Health New Zealand and the government.
Two others are being worked on.
This year, in 2026, the Minister for Mental Health will
undertake a review of our effectiveness that was built into the legislation from
the beginning and the operations of Te Hiringa Māhara.
Our case studies highlight the system is shifting.
We are a trusted voice in speaking up on important health and mental health,
addiction, and wellbeing issues.
We recognize much more needs to be done and will take time,
sustained leadership, and action.
The new mental health and wellbeing strategy and our ongoing oversight,
monitoring, and advocacy will be critical to seeing the change
people and whanau want to see. Tena koutou.
Thank you. Thanks very much for that.
Some questions. Ingrid Lari.
Yeah. Thank you very much, and lovely to see you all.
I'm interested to pick up some of the themes that you mentioned
in your introduction, and
clear that,
when you say a trusted voice, that certainly is the perceptions in the sector.
So I'm curious to know some things from a systems perspective that
your commission has highlighted.
One of the things we asked you about a number of years ago was
leadership in the mental health sector, and I'm wanting to get a sense from you
whether
you feel that the leadership is there.
And I'm thinking also about accountability, given that we have more agencies
who
are supposed to or have a statutory duty to provide some accountability.
But when we ask them all, fingers go the other way, or there
just isn't the legislative teeth.
And in fact, when I look, I see accountability's only really at a contracted
level, which are very output-orientated.
So there's two systems levels questions there. One is, is the leadership there?
Secondly, are you satisfied that the accountabilities are baked into the system in
the way they should be around mental health?
Let me start. I think we can all probably have a bit of a go at that question.
It's a big one, Ingrid. So,
in terms of leadership, we've seen some change and
improvement, particularly within Health New Zealand.
And we've been able to actively engage
at the governance and senior
operational level. So we've seen
a deficit, I suppose, of leadership in that space that has been being filled in
recent times.
In terms of
accountability, I think there's still ways to go.
We think that the mental health and wellbeing strategy is an important one, that
it has an overlay that includes within it
milestones and accountabilities that you refer
to.
I can give one example of where system
change leadership where we particularly played a
role, and that's in the crisis response space.
There was a lack of clear leadership across the various different agencies,
police, justice, and health,
and we managed to fill a void. And I think that as a result of that, there was some
progress made, and then government subsequently making some investments.
So, change is happening in pockets, but I would still
say in terms of overall accountability in a joined up system, we have a
long way to go. And as I said in that introduction, it's going to require time and
sustained leadership for us to get there.
That's a starter. Or Karen.
Could I sleep on that, Mr. Chair?
Of course you can.
Thank you for that. And I guess that leads me into
a pivot, if you like, or a focus now on youth.
And I know that in your latest report, we're still seeing
youth having longer wait times to access specialist services.
And in fact, we're seeing longer wait times for addiction services as well.
So I'm interested to know whether the focus on youth is--
whether you're able, within the current architecture, to include some of the really
important things like social determinants and other elements that might
make a significant difference to lifting that access and engagement.
I think there's two parts to your question.
In terms of the first part of your
question...
Yes. Sure thing.
Just sorry, can you
clarify that first part?
So the first part is around the wait times for-
Yes. Sorry. Wait times
... 19 to 24-year-olds.
So if we're unpicking it, I think is probably the best
one-line statement we can say.
What we're finding in for young people is the referrals are being made, but they're
being declined, and so understanding what that's about is
important to us.
Is that work happening? Do you know the tracking of the declines?
Karen.
Yeah. Okay, I can comment on that. So we've had a long-term focus on
rangatahi and young people, looking at both what are the drivers
for the increasing rates of distress, in that sort of determinant
space. And we reported on the wellbeing outcomes for rangatahi and youth,
as well as access to services. So both is really important.
You've seen youth reflected in our crisis and other
work, and we are doing a deep dive this year to really try and
understand those pathways across the system, but
also the access patterns. So what we have seen is, as Hayden said,
a continued decline in the numbers of young people accessing specialist
services. And there are more options in primary and community, but we're
still hearing a lot about the concerns that people have about young people not
being able to get access when they need it.
So we need to understand how that whole system's working.
Yeah, I'll just leave it there.
She's on, she-
Yeah.
Oh, yeah. But I want a sup on the second round.
Thank you.
Before a sup, and then it's Kahu.
Yeah, just final sup. Thank you for that.
Last night when I asked the minister about this, he said that
people are being referred to different organizations or different specialists, and
that's why there are these declines.
Have you seen evidence that young people are actually being deferred
into services and receiving the treatment, or is that a statement that
needs some evidence behind it?
Yeah. Look, we have heard that
referrals are being declined because there's more appropriate
services, and we know there are more options in those primary and
community services, particularly like the youth stream of
access and choice and other options.
We don't fully understand the detail that sits behind that,
which is what we're hoping to understand more in the deep dive that we're doing
this year.
So there is something happening in terms of that 18 to 24
age group who are accessing adult services.
So wanting to understand what's happening within services and
why young people, that age group, has continued to decline while adults
has increased. So there is something under there that we need to understand,
so we're looking at it.
Which is what my sup was going to be about, is that transition from youth to
adult and
being able to understand what is the gap or the blockage
in that transition. If you have any comment on that.
Yeah. Look, we haven't particularly looked at
that transition, and so that is part of the work we're doing this year,
as part of the deep dive, looking at young people and how
that transition works. Because we have heard many...
Young people are all different, and so for some, they may be more ready to
transition into adult services. Others might prefer to
actually be continued to be supported in the services they're currently
receiving. So as I said, that's what we're looking at this year.
Right. So you're going to look at that to understand what that
blockage or-
Yes.
Mm.
Yes. And so in our work, what we do is we have the broad brush approach to
monitoring, and then we highlight the areas where we need to have a closer look.
And last year, it was really clear that crisis responses was an area we
needed to have a closer look.
So we did that deep dive last year. This year, it's really having a
look at those youth services.
And that's how we work as an entity, is to say, "Where do we need to have a closer
look?" And do that work.
So that there's a gap in that transition bit at the moment.
Yeah.
That's what we need to understand more.
Yeah. And that's where we add as value to the system, is being able to look at
those deep dives,
bring some international evidence, what is working well in New Zealand,
what can we learn across the system. So that's what we're doing.
I-
Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you for being here and for your mahi.
We know that our rangatahi have some of the worst statistics for mental
health
in the OECD, and I want to talk about
your advocacy role, particularly with other ministries and
ministers. So we know that the new
Minister Stanford's new curriculum,
particularly the health and physical education curriculum, does not
mention the words mental health at all.
How have you advocated to the minister or the Ministry for
Education to have mental health within the curriculum?
Yeah. We haven't had any specific advocacy
to the minister. When we have those relationships across
government, and certainly we know that other government entities,
agencies, draw from our work, and so we
certainly advocate for youth voice, so for young people to have a voice,
in whatever changes are being proposed.
But also for young people to have greater knowledge about
mental health and wellbeing, what they can do to keep themselves well
and safe. So yeah, we certainly advocate for that.
Thank you. Supplementary.
And so do you think it is vital for
youth and rangatahi to get support, information, and understanding through their
education
at school?
Yeah. Look, this is a personal response. Yes.
Have we done any particular work in a specific area?
We haven't.
And supplementary. And so has the Ministry for Education or
the Minister of Education reached out to you for that advice?
I'm not aware of that.
Thank you. And one more,
if that's okay.
It's okay.
Thank you.
So polite. Has there been any discussion
around or thoughts around having a cross-ministry
group that meets, like the group that was set up after
Te Aorerekura for the prevention of family and sexual
violence was set up, where they came together, talked
about mental health, particularly around youth,
for this case. Has there been any work or thoughts done
around that?
With the focus on education and wellbeing?
With a focus on youth mental health.
Not that I'm aware of. Yeah.
Do you think that-
We've-
Yeah.
The closest piece of work that we've been involved in there would be in the
crisis response space.
Mm.
And there have been some
short-term ministry-led initiatives, but not specifically on this
kaupapa.
Mm.
There has been work across government in previous
terms of government with drawing together different
ministers with different portfolios to focus on
the child and youth wellbeing. Yeah.
Thank you.
Hamish.
Yes, sir. Bring it. Thank you.
During the year, you've updated your strategy.
What is the main shifts in your priorities, and what are they based on?
Good question.
I think a number of factors
came to play. I'll start the ball rolling, but a number of factors came to
play for us. One,
we
found our straps, I suppose you could say, being a startup 60 years
ago. So we were clear about what our purpose was, and
we were clear about the resources that we had.
The other part, of course, is that we utilized the public service to assist us
in engaging with stakeholders. So we
listened to a lot of people,
in effect, and helped shape our strategy.
So knowing what we were about, knowing the resources that we had, knowing what
we heard, and that brought us into identifying
six areas to shift where from to.
And I think I'm going to ask Karen just to cover off on those three
areas, but they're quite important for us in terms of driving the
decisions that we make at the board table and elsewhere inside the organization.
So last year, we did undertake what's called a future excellence horizon, which is
common across government. So we
interviewed a range of stakeholders and also
conducted a survey to get feedback from people about if we were the best
organization we could be, what that would look like.
And so what we heard was that we needed to place people's
lived experience much more centered in our work.
We needed to work to improve a fragmented system.
So we'd heard a lot about the fragmentation and the lack of leadership.
We needed to lift our profile, so we heard quite a lot about our visibility,
and that we needed to look ahead and think about the demographic changes that were
happening. And so that then led into the refocus
of our strategy, and our 24/25 annual
report reports against our previous four strategic priorities.
So what we're now working to is a refined
set of strategic priorities, which is about focusing on
people with lived experience, people who are using mental health and addiction
services,
so much more focused on services.
Continuing to have a focus on broader wellbeing outcomes and
determinants. And the new one is really how we look at the performance
of the mental health and addiction system.
And, as we've mentioned previously, we did release our first
system performance report last year.
We are about to release our second one this year in
June, and that is where the six shifts that we identified.
So we said that the system needs to shift in these ways,
and that's really informed the mental health and wellbeing strategy.
So, happy to take further questions on that.
Just a quick set. In multiple of your
answers, you've talked about your stakeholders.
Who do you see your stakeholders? Who do you see those people or
stakeholders? Who are they?
Oh, look, they're really broad.
We have multiple stakeholders. So of course, it's people who have lived
experience and the whanau, families who support them,
and that's a really broad and diverse group.
We have obligations to Maori and whanau as part of
our
legislation and how we engage with Maori and whanau and prioritize
equity for Maori. We have some really important sector stakeholders,
so working with people who are providing services, commissioning
services to achieve the changes that we want to see
across government. We also have
stakeholders in terms of what we describe as the priority populations.
So in our act, there are a list of 12 populations
who have a higher prevalence of distress,
and so it's important that we connect with those groups as well.
So Maori, Pacific, young people, veterans is the
list. So it's a really broad group, and we're a small entity of around 20
staff. So we have to really think smartly about how we
connect in with
organizations that represent those populations and with the
providers, so both Health New Zealand and NGOs, other
providers. So it's a big challenge for us, how we keep that
connection.
I appreciate you've got your priority
populations, and I think last year when you were here, I did ask
about middle-aged males.
Yes.
They're the group that are most likely to die
by suicide. What are you doing to reach out to
stakeholders in that group?
Yeah. So-
Because I suppose one of my fears is that some of these people aren't
accessing mental health support.
Yeah.
And so probably very hard for you to pick up because they're not
engaging with services.
Yeah. And look, I think that's
the relationships we have with other agencies and
organizations who
are connected with those groups. Because it's hard for
us to have the direct reach into those communities, given the nature of our
organization and our size. So we do have
that intentional engagement with the groups that represent,
so be it the rural sector or if
it's Pacific peoples.
So how do we have those connections?
And then also more broadly, like the Mental Health Foundation and those
organizations.
Yes. And just one quick follow-up.
Hayden, during your initial
briefing, you mentioned that case studies show that the system is
shifting. Is that in relation to these priorities, or
were you meaning something different?
I was
referring to the areas that we've seen in the legislation,
the taking up of the
strategy for mental health and wellbeing,
focus on youth, and we're also talking about access and
choice. So those are the areas that we particularlyWhere we've worked on,
where we can see some contribution to where those areas of
work have shifted.
A supp on that.
Definitely.
True.
Move a supp on that.
Yeah. Just to clarify, the system shift you're talking about are
mainly inputs in terms of legislative change and systems
change rather than outcomes, which would take some time to
measure.
Of course.
Thank you.
Yes. Quite right. Yeah.
Right. Carlos.
The supp or primary?
Supplementary.
Why do you have a-
Yes
... supplementary? I've got you down as a primary. You're after Carlos.
You're next. Carlos.
Thank you, Chair. I read your statement of intent
that was published in last year, July.
Mm-hmm.
But I couldn't find anything that talk about the needs of the AFEX community.
In the past five years, the risk of mental health issues
like depression have been significantly increased in
Asian communities.
Mm-hmm.
Do you think this is a serious concern that need more
attention?
Mm-hmm. Yes, we do. We're not
reflecting necessarily all of that work that's been done, but
there's some work that's been done through Karen and the team, and that will,
in time,
surface. But perhaps Karen's in a better position to articulate
the work that we're doing.
Yes.
I mean, if you think it is a very serious concern, why not a single
wording or single strategies have been addressing
the needs of our Asian community?
It probably reflects the fact that we weren't there at that
particular time. But it's not to say...
It's basically saying
we can't get to everything at once.
But having said that, we're now in a position, and we are starting to
move into making connections in that particular area.
So you're trying to tell me that you don't think there's a needs for our
Asian community before?
We knew that.
You knew, but you didn't react.
Well, again, a small organization trying to do the
best that we can in terms of reaching.
If you look at all 12 population groups, they're quite extensive and
require quite
a lot of effort and resource to engage in an appropriate way and follow
through. So,
at that particular time, we clearly weren't in a position to
articulate anything. We are in a better position to do that now.
All right. So what do you think are the main reason affecting the mental health in
our AFEX community? How does the Mental Health and Wellbeing
Commission support a speaker for them?
So, I've had a lot of engagement, particularly with Asian Family
Services, and so we have supported the
summits that Kelly and her team have hosted, both in
Auckland and then the one in
Parliament. And we do look at how we reflect
Asian communities, Asian population in our substantive
reports. So for example, in our access and choice report, we
highlighted the needs for Asian people to have access to those access and choice
services. And in our other reports, we also highlight what
are the needs for Asian people alongside Maori and
Pacific. So we certainly have done work to
connect with
both Asian Family Services and other key leaders from
the Asian community, and to reflect that in our work.
Mr Chair, have you removed the barrier for the Asian
community to access to mental health services?
Have we removed the barrier?
Any barrier.
Oh, I think-
Or you just believe that there's no barrier for any
AFEX groups or Asian communities to access to the
mental health service?
Our role is to highlight what's happening in terms of access
and need, and then to advocate
for that. And
in terms of for the Asian population, we have been reflecting
those needs and doing that advocacy in our work.
And I think this does highlight the challenge, as Hayden said.
There are 12 priority populations, and it's hard to reflect
each of those populations specifically, particularly in a statement of
intent, which is around us as an entity.
Mr Chair. One more.
But in our substantive work, we certainly do everything to bring forward those
voices in our work.
If you
just mentioned that your main function is to advocate
for people or give advices, what advice have you given to the
minister so far to address the needs of our Asian
communities? And also, are you happy with the result
you have been implementing the last 12 months so far?
If not, which areas do you think it needs improve?
We have raised
the issue with the minister. We haven't got a clear pathway forward, but we have
raised it as an important area that needs attention.
And specifically in relation to the access and choice
program and their report at the end of five years.
And we did highlight the need for a service stream that was
specific for the Asian population.
Just a quick comment.
And the minister responded to that in a positive way.
Just a last comment.
Hold on, Carlos.
Yeah, just a quick comment, yeah.
You have a quick comment, and then I'm going to Ingrid for a supp, and Cam also has
a supp.
Just let you know I'm very dis-
No, hold on, Carlos. And then Kahu has her primary.
Oh, sorry. It was a supplementary, not a primary. Thank you.
Quick comment.
Just let you know, I'm very disappointed with the answer you provide today, and I
expect a better outcome in the next 12 months.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Supplementary.
Yeah.
Ingrid Leary.
Thank you for that. Speaking to Dr.
Kelly Feng, it seems that one of the problems is that you don't
know what you don't know when it comes to the Asian-
But mental health issues and gambling in particular.
So first, is that a piece of work that is-- when you talk about the work
that you're doing, Karen, is that what you're looking at?
Because that baseline appears to be missing, and that's probably a big part
of why they don't get funding that I think Dr
Chung is alluding to is not proportionate to the problem.
We can't count it. The second part is with access and choice.
Are you aware that the access and choice solution provided by the government
is not the same as the other strands of access and choice, is
convoluted, and therefore makes it really problematic for Asian communities
to actually access access and choice?
And if so, what would you like to see done about that?
Yeah.
So just on that second part,
I am aware that the solution for the Asian stream
of access and choice is different from the other streams, and that reflects
that those other streams were right from the beginning, and then this is a
solution to improve access. And I know that's, I haven't talked
with Kelly recently, but some aspects of that are quite challenging,
and those do need to be worked through.
I think ultimately it is about people being able to access the services and
supports in a timely way. So it is about the specific streams, and it
is also about the appropriateness of all services for
people who are accessing those services.
The
Asian population is
made up of different types of population, as it is for Pacific peoples
as well. So it is how do we, yeah, as said, have the specific
streams and services that meet everybody's needs in the most appropriate way.
And then, so is it baselining of the how big is the problem, which is the
question, and is difficult if the data is only being collected from
small data sets of who is accessing-
Yes
... and what we're seeing on the ground is a much bigger problem.
Is that a gap in the system, and is that something that you are looking at, or?
Yeah, and that goes to the prevalence study.
Mm-hmm.
So we have advocated for a proper sort of study of the level of
need in communities. And we're really pleased-
Speaking particularly about the Asian community.
Yes. Well, that would include what's the level of need for
Asian communities as well as other communities.
So the youth stream is underway, and that should give us a
really good insight into what is the level of need for
all of those populations, and then how can services be targeted
to meet those needs. And particularly for the youth does pick up,
that's part of those changing demographics.
So, you see that particularly in that age group.
So that will give us some really useful information.
But not only us, for the whole system.
So we have a role to play-
Mm-hmm
... but we're not the commissioners and funders of services.
So we're bringing that oversight.
Yeah.
So, we have views and insights, and we can feed those in
with.
I think you can say that we are increasing our presence in this area, and
you will see more from us.
Thank you.
Karen.
Oh, thank you, Chair.
Supplementary.
Cheers.
I heard there's a supplementary then you ask for your primary.
The questions that we've had in this
line of question, we have focused on groups of people,
specifically. Heard some
strong advocacy and questions from Dr Chung
on Asian,
Dr Campbell on middle-aged men. Do you think that
labeling, listing, categorizing the way it's done in this act, in your
enabling piece of legislation, is actually
making your job harder
when a population is divided up and everyone starts fighting from ?
Do you think that it's actually the amount of trust in what you're able
to do is being impacted because of the
dividing people up and clear breach of a social contract where we all come
together to support each other?
Or do you think politicians are dividing us?
Yeah, look, I think-
Like, is it making your job-
I think it's-
... easier or harder?
I think it's helpful-
Speaking to the-
... to be-
... base
I think if we're looking at it from
a general how we apply out the resources that we have,
it gives us, it recognizes that not one shoe
or one solution fits all.
But it does provide a challenge for us when it comes to applying that, and
practically,
there are some challenges for us to be able to move effectively across all
of those bases.
Is that because there's contradictions in the act between things like,
I think it's Section 10, where you can, as a commission, can decide
populations you want to focus on, but then also you've got Clause
13, Schedule 2 with these lists of populations you have to
justify,
actually looking at wider population bases.
I
guess I'm trying to figure out, like, we've heard some emotion.
I would say there's a lot of people who would read this legislation, and yeah, you
could say it's a politician, and say, are you actually able to
look at the population and identify,
based on
what you find or do you feel directed by this to these
population bases?
Mm-hmm. If I could add a comment, because it is one of the shifts that we've made
in our strategy. So when we started in the early
period of time, we did do a report focused on the wellbeing outcomes
